structure.title: A transformer provides well defined function signatures per each communication relation (port based and signal based), which is marked for transformation. The function signature depends on the transmitted data elements (Client/Server operation signature or Sender/Receiver interface signature) only. The output of a transformer will be always a linear byte array.

A more powerful system can chain multiple transformers where the input of the first transformer in the chain gets the data from the RTE. Each following transformer uses the output of the preceding transformer as input. All transformers following the first one then have generic signature with just a byte array as IN and OUT parameter. Such an architecture could be used to design systems, where you can flexibly add functionality like safety or security protection to a serialized stream.
2 Acronyms and Abbreviations

nodes.title: 3.1 Input documents
nodes.summary: The input documents for section 3.1 include the Glossary (AUTOSAR\_FO\_TR\_Glossary), General Specification of Basic Software Modules (AUTOSAR\_CP\_SWS\_BSWGeral), System Template (AUTOSAR\_CP\_TPS\_SystemTemplate), General Requirements on Basic Software Modules (AUTOSAR\_CP\_RS\_BSWGeral), and Software Component Template (AUTOSAR\_CP\_TPS\_SoftwareComponentTemplate). These documents provide foundational definitions, specifications, and templates for developing and structuring basic software modules in AUTOSAR. Each document is referenced by its specific identifier to ensure clarity and traceability within the technical framework. The listed documents collectively define the requirements and structural guidelines for software components and system templates. No additional information beyond the listed documents is included.

nodes.title: 4.1 Limitations
nodes.summary: The "Limitations" section highlights that data transformation and communication complexity necessitate restrictions due to diverse use cases and their impact on transformer functionality, particularly in the RTE. Transforming large data elements is efficient for Ethernet (large PDU) but inefficient for CAN (small PDU) due to fragmented serialization. Only specific data elements (VariableDataPrototypes), operations (ClientServerOperations), and trigger events (non-queued) are subject to transformation

nodes.title: 4.2 Applicability to car domains
nodes.summary: The section "4.2 Applicability to car domains" states that no restrictions apply, indicating the described technology or standard is fully compatible with car domains without limitations. This implies the solution can be implemented across all relevant automotive applications without constraints. No specific exclusions, prerequisites, or conditional requirements are mentioned. The phrasing suggests universal applicability within the automotive context. No further technical details or exceptions are provided in the text.

structure.title: 5 Dependencies to other modules
structure.summary: The section "5 Dependencies to other modules" explicitly states that there are no dependencies on AUTOSAR SWS modules. This indicates the described module does not require any components or functionalities from the AUTOSAR SWS modules for its operation. All technical details are confined to the absence of such dependencies, with no additional module interactions or requirements mentioned. The summary preserves the original text's precision and scope without extrapolation.

nodes.title: 5.1.1  file structure
nodes.summary: The code file structure for transformers is defined by [2, SWS BSW General], which establishes that all transformers are BSW modules. This standard applies uniformly to all transformer implementations. Deviations from this structure are explicitly documented in the specific SWS documents associated with individual transformers. The reference [2, SWS BSW General] serves as the foundational framework for organizing transformer code. No additional deviations or exceptions are mentioned beyond those specified in the relevant SWS documents.

structure.title: 6 Requirements Tracing
structure.summary: The "Requirements Tracing" section maps SRS (Software Requirements Specification) requirements to corresponding SWS (Software Specification) requirements, detailing how each SRS requirement is fulfilled by one or more SWS entries. Two tables list 11 SRS requirements (e.g., SRS_Xfrm_00001 to SRS_Xfrm_00011) and their satisfied SWS counterparts, such as SWS_Xfrm_00017 for SRS_Xfrm_00001. Each SRS requirement is explicitly linked to specific SWS IDs, with some requiring multiple SWS entries (e.g., SRS_Xfrm_00002 references 18 SWS requirements). The section ensures traceability between high-level SRS specifications and detailed SWS implementation requirements.

structure.title: A transformers takes data from the RTE, works on them and returns the output back to the RTE. It can both serialize/linearize data (transform them from a structured into a linear form) and transform (modify or extend linear data) them (e.g add a checksum).
structure.summary: Transformers are BSW modules within the Communication Service Cluster, providing communication services to the RTE. They are executed by the RTE when the RTE requires the specific service they offer. These modules process data by either serializing/linearizing structured data into a linear form or transforming linear data (e.g., adding a checksum). The RTE initiates the execution of transformers to handle data transformations and ensure proper communication service delivery.

structure.title: A transformer is no library because transformers can hold an internal state but they can work as well stateless.
structure.summary: A transformer is not a library because it can maintain internal state, though it may operate statelessly. Stateful transformers cannot be used as generic libraries, but they can be integrated into chains. Transformer chains are coordinated by the RTE, executed in the specified order, and configured via the [3, System Template], with a maximum length of 255 transformers. The sending side’s transformer order is inverted on the receiving side, with retransformers applied in reverse. Transformers must specify a fixed serialized output format to ensure compatibility with remote ECUs or BSW modules

structure.title: A further example of data transformation is shown in Figure 7.2. Here the use-case of intra-ECU data transformation is addressed.
structure.summary: The section describes an example of intra-ECU data transformation (Figure 7.2) where a transformer must address architectural differences between sender and target ECUs, such as bit-width (8/16/32-bit), endianness, and multi-byte word ordering. It emphasizes defining data semantics (e.g., two’s complement, character encoding) and source/target data types based on connected PortPrototype/SystemSignal. Padding rules and data element ordering in complex data are also explicitly required. All these specifications are static and can be hardcoded into the transformer implementation during RTE generation.

structure.title: A transformer gets its input data via a pointer which destination can vary in length. Therefore, an implementation of a transformer has to cope with input data which are longer than expected.
structure.summary: A transformer must handle input data via a pointer with variable lengths, tolerating unexpected data while

structure.title: A transformer will usually work on the data and/or generate some protocol information which are stored in a header and/or footer of the output. Therefore it needs a place to write the result to. Transformers can work with two buffer handling modes: In-place buffer and out-of-place buffer. Which one is used is determined by the configuration in the [3, System Template] and influences the transformer’s interface.
structure.summary: Transformers operate using in-place or out-of-place buffering, determined by the [3, System Template] configuration, which affects their interface. In-place buffering reuses the input buffer as output, requiring a single buffer pointer argument and modifying the input data, while out-of-place buffering uses separate input and output buffers, preserving the input data. The Rte allocates buffers and calculates required sizes for output, ensuring compliance with constraints like non-modifiable input data. Transformers in specific

nodes.title: 7.2 Transformer Classes
nodes.summary: The section explains that transformers are classified into different classes due to their diverse functional purposes. It highlights that various types of transformers exist, each designed to fulfill distinct operational requirements. This categorization into classes reflects the necessity to differentiate transformers based on their specific roles and functionalities. The text emphasizes that the classification system arises from the wide range of transformer applications. No further technical details or external information are provided in the original text.

structure.title: A transformer class shall contain all transformers which provide similar functionality. At most one transformer of each transformer class shall be allowed per transformer chain.

A serializer transformer accepts complex data (either a Sender/Receiver data element or a Client/Server operation with its arguments) or no data (Trigger communication) from the RTE and provides the resulting byte array as an <u>ISignal</u> or part of <u>IPdu</u>, which is finally transmitted to the receiver by the COM stack.
A safety transformer protects the communication against unintentional modifications to ensure a safe data transmission.
structure.summary: The safety transformer is mandated to protect inter-ECU communication for safety-related SWCs, ensuring correct data transmission order and content. It must fulfill safety requirements by mechanisms such as sequence counters and checksums. The upstream requirement SRS_Xfrm_00001 specifies these obligations, emphasizing protection against unintentional modifications. The transformer's role includes maintaining data integrity and transmission sequence to guarantee safe data transfer. Examples of implementation include cryptographic checks and sequence number validation.

structure.title: A security transformer protects the communication against intentional modifications to ensure security of the bus communication.
structure.summary: The security transformer is required to protect inter-ECU communication for security-related SWCs, ensuring data authenticity, integrity, and freshness. These requirements are fulfilled through mechanisms like sequence counters and checksums, which address the specified security needs. The transformer's role is critical in safeguarding bus communication against intentional modifications. The upstream requirements (SRS_Xfrm_00001) explicitly mandate these protections. The example methods align with the technical details outlined in the requirements.

nodes.title: 7.2.4 Custom
nodes.summary: Custom transformers are not standardized by AUTOSAR but can be defined by any development party to address non-standardized transformation needs. They are implemented as CDDs (Consistency Diagnostic Definitions), which are part of the AUTOSAR framework. This approach allows flexibility for implementing custom logic outside standardized transformer specifications. The use of CDDs enables customization while adhering to AUTOSAR's structural requirements. No additional constraints or details about implementation methods are provided in the text.

nodes.title: 7.3 Error Handling
nodes.summary: Transformers return errors to the RTE as return codes, which determines whether the transformer chain continues or aborts. Errors are categorized into soft (0x01–0x7F) and hard (0x80–0xFF) errors, with soft errors allowing chain continuation and hard errors triggering abortion due to severe issues. Hard errors require the output buffer to remain unchanged, while soft errors may signal warnings. Each transformer class has a predefined error set, and transformers must only return errors within their

structure.title: AUTOSAR CP R25-11
structure.summary: The section highlights that soft errors in early transformer stages of an AUTOSAR CP R25-11 implementation may be masked by subsequent hard errors in later transformers. For example, the E2E transformer may trigger a soft error upon detecting a corrupted or masqueraded message, but the SomelpXf transformer could override this with a hard error if deserialization fails, thereby obscuring the E2E state machine's state transition. The E2E state machine remains in the INVALID state until valid messages are processed, ensuring the application observes this state once deserialization succeeds. Applications relying solely on the E2E state machine must explicitly evaluate deserializer hard errors to avoid misinterpretation of error conditions.

structure.title: **[SWS\_Xfrm\_00031] Errors of serializer transformers** Upstream requirements: SRS\_Xfrm\_00010
structure.summary: The section outlines error codes for serializer transformers, categorized into **Soft** (recoverable) and **Hard** (non-recoverable) errors. **E_OK (0x00)** indicates success, while **E_NO_DATA (0x01)** signals no deserializable data. **E_SER_PAYLOAD_LENGTH_EXCEEDED (0

nodes.title: 7.3.2 Errors of Safety Transformers
nodes.summary: The "Errors of Safety Transformers" section outlines error codes for safety transformer communication, categorized under "Soft" error types. Errors related to data validity (e.g., E_SAFETY_VALID_REP, E_SAFETY_VALID_SEQ) indicate data compliance with safety checks despite issues like repeated counters or counter jumps. No-data errors (e.g., E_SAFETY_NODATA_OK, E_SAFETY_NODATA_SEQ) occur when no valid data is received since initialization, often due to counter anomalies or failed checks. Invalid data errors (e.g., E_SAFETY_INVALID_REP, E_SAFETY_INVALID_ERR) signify data rejection

nodes.title: 7.3.3 Errors of Security Transformers
nodes.summary: The section outlines error codes for security transformers, noting that values 0x04, 0x24, 0x34, and 0x44 are reserved for internal E2E Library use. It lists errors categorized as "Soft" or "Hard," with descriptions detailing communication validity, state machine states, and safety checks. Key errors include E_SAFETY_INVALID_NND (0x45) for invalid communication, E_SAFETY_NOSM_ERR (0x63) for failed data checks, and E_SAFETY_HARD_RUNTIMEERROR (0xFF) for critical runtime failures. Additional errors like E_OK (0x00) and E_SEC_NOT_AUTH (0x01) address authentication and freshness issues, with soft errors typically disabling E2E state machines while hard errors indicate severe safety violations.

nodes.title: 7.4.1 Development Errors
nodes.summary: The section defines development errors in module Xfrm, referencing upstream requirement SRS_BSW_00337. It specifies that the Module Implementation Prefix (MIP) of the transformer, as defined in [SWS_BSW_00102], must be entirely written in uppercase letters. The MIP is a critical component in identifying and addressing development errors within the Xfrm module. No additional details about error types or handling are provided in this

nodes.title: 7.4.3 Production Errors
nodes.summary: The section states there are no production errors, but defines error codes for transformer-specific issues. Soft errors (0x01–0x7F) and hard errors (0x80–0xFF) are categorized by severity. Additional error codes include <MIP>_E_UNINIT (0x01) for uninitialized modules, <MIP>_E_INIT_FAILED (0x02) for invalid configurations, <MIP>_E_PARAM (0x03) for incorrect parameters, and <MIP>_E_PARAM_POINTER (0x04) for invalid pointers. These codes are tied to specific API usage conditions.

nodes.title: 7.4.4 Extended Production Errors
nodes.summary: The section titled "7.4.4 Extended Production Errors" outlines the list and specifications of Extended Production Errors for transformers. It serves as a reference chapter detailing these errors, which are likely related to manufacturing or operational defects in transformer production. The content focuses on categorizing and defining such errors without elaborating on specific error types or corrective measures. The summary emphasizes the chapter's role in documenting these errors for technical clarity and compliance.

nodes.title: 7.4.4.1 XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE
nodes.summary: The section defines the Extended Production Error **XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE**, which must exist for every transformer where the error code **_XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE** is set. This requirement is governed by the upstream specifications **SRS_BSW_00466** and **SRS_BSW_00469**. The error is triggered when a transformer encounters a malformed message, ensuring compliance with defined error-handling protocols. The error code is explicitly tied to the transformer's configuration and operational constraints. No additional details about error resolution or triggers are provided in

nodes.title: 7.5 Error Notification
nodes.summary: The error **XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE** occurs when a transformer fails to produce output due to malformed input data. The long description clarifies that the input data does not meet the specific transformer's format requirements, preventing successful processing. Detection criteria distinguish between a **Fail** (input format non-conformance) and **Pass** (input format conformance). Monitoring occurs **on every transformer execution**, with no secondary parameters or time requirements specified.

nodes.title: 8.1 Imported types
nodes.summary: The section "8.1 Imported types" specifies that transformers must use the `ImplementationDataTypes` defined by RTE, as mandated by upstream requirement SRS_Xfrm_00002. These types are sourced from the Module Interlink Types Header file, which is referenced in SWS_Rte_07503. The requirement ensures consistency by binding the transformer’s data types to RTE’s definitions. No additional details about the header file’s structure or implementation are provided. The focus is on adherence to predefined types for interoperability.

nodes.title: 8.3 Function definitions
nodes.summary:
The "Function definitions" section outlines the naming conventions for transformer IDs in the BswModuleEntry, governed by the presence of XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef and specific Com module configurations. When no XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef exists, transformerIds follow patterns like Com_, Com_, or Com_, depending on ComIPduLargeData settings. If XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef is present, the name uses _

_, derived from SwComponentPrototype, PortPrototype, and VariableDataPrototype short names. The transformer function signature depends on XfrmVariableDataPrototypeInstanceRef, potentially generating multiple functions for a single XfrmSignal if referenced by multiple XfrmImplementationMappings. These patterns prioritize readability

nodes.summary: The function **\<Mip\>\_ExtractProtocolHeaderFields** (S

nodes.title: 8.3.2 \<Mip\>\_<transformerId\>
nodes.summary: The `<Mip>_<transformerId>` function is a synchronous, reentrant service in the sender-side transformer chain for Sender/Receiver communication, defined in `<Mip>.h`. It transforms data elements using the `dataElement` parameter, storing results in the `buffer` and returning the length via `bufferLength`. The function returns `uint8` values (0x00 for success, 0x01–0xff for errors) and relies on `Std_ReturnType` to indicate parsing success (`E_OK`) or failure (`E_NOT_OK`). It is required as the first transformer in a chain when a `DataTransformation` is linked to a `TransformationTechnology` via specific mappings

nodes.title: 8.3.3 \<Mip\>\_Inv\_\<transformerId\>
nodes.summary: The service `<Mip>_Inv_<transformerId>` is a synchronous, reentrant function with Service ID 0x04, used as the first transformer in a `transformerChain` for data transformation in Sender/Receiver communication. It processes serialized data via a buffer and bufferLength, deserializing into the `dataElement` parameter, which may require updates for variable-size arrays using `pavloadAsPointerToArray`. The function returns `E_OK` (0x00) for success or error codes (0x01–0xff) and is defined in `<Mip>.h`. It is required when a

structure.summary: The "Mip" (Module Implementation Prefix) is a component of the transformer's structure as defined in the AUTOSAR CP R25-11 specification. The Mip serves as a naming convention for module implementations within the transformer's context. The term is directly tied to the standard's requirements for module identification and organization. structure.title: 10 Configuration specification
structure.summary: This section outlines configuration parameters and their organization into containers, with Chapter 10.1 providing foundational details and a template for parameter specification, retained to ensure clarity. Section 10.2 defines the structure and parameters of transformers. Transformers are configured at the system level using [3, System Template] and at the software component level via [5, Software Component Template]. These configurations enable the generation of a basic **EcuC** for the transformer. The chapter emphasizes structured parameter definition and hierarchical organization for system-level configuration.

nodes.title: 10.2 Containers and configuration parameters
nodes.summary: The section outlines configuration parameters for a general transformer setup, with detailed explanations referenced in Chapter 7 (Functional Specification) and Chapter 8 (API specification). Specific transformers utilize the EcuC container, which is populated with their specific contents. The EcuC is generated automatically from the DataTransformationSet, as the transformer generator possesses all required information. The parameter **[ECUC_Xfrm_00014]** defines the EcucModuleDef Xfrm, specifying its role in the configuration.

nodes.title: 10.2.1 XfrmGeneral
nodes.summary: The **XfrmGeneral** container, part of the Xfrm module, contains general configuration parameters for the module. It is a single-instance container (multiplicity 1) under the Xfrm parent container. The module does not support post-build variant configuration and is pre-compiled. Key parameters include **XfrmDevErrorDetect**, a boolean that enables or disables development error detection (default: false), and other parameters like **XfrmInstanceld** and **XfrmVersionInfoApi**

10.2.3 XfrmSignal
nodes.summary: The **XfrmSignal** container, part of the **XfrmImplementationMapping** hierarchy, references a signal in the system description that transports transformed data. It has a multiplicity of 0..1, indicating it is optional and may appear once or not at all. The container includes a **XfrmSignalChoice** sub-container with a multiplicity of .1, specifying whether an ISi Signal or an ISi SignalGroup is referenced.
